resume:
PRIYA NARAYANAN
Supply Chain & Operations Intern Candidate | Final-year B.S. Industrial & Systems Engineering

SUMMARY
Final-year Industrial & Systems Engineering student with two completed internships in distribution and one semester-long capstone embedded with a regional grocery distributor. My focus has been on the unglamorous middle of the supply chain: inbound dock scheduling, slotting, cycle counting, and the messy reality of why a forecast and a shelf rarely agree. I am most comfortable with a clipboard on a warehouse floor in the morning and a pivot table in the afternoon. I am applying for the Supply Chain Operations Intern role because I want a rotation that sits between planning and execution, where I can keep learning how decisions made in a planning system actually land on a dock door.

EDUCATION
B.S. Industrial & Systems Engineering, expected May next year. Relevant coursework: Deterministic Operations Research, Stochastic Models, Inventory Theory, Quality Engineering (SPC), Supply Chain Network Design, and a Lean/Six Sigma practicum. Cumulative GPA 3.6. I also completed an elective in database systems, which is where I learned enough SQL to stop bothering the analytics team for every data pull.

INTERNSHIP EXPERIENCE

Distribution Operations Intern — Regional Grocery Distributor (last summer, 12 weeks)
This was a full-time summer internship at a perishables distribution center serving roughly 140 grocery stores. I reported to the inbound operations supervisor and spent the first three weeks just learning the floor — receiving, putaway, replenishment, and the cold-chain handoffs. The core of my summer was a project on inbound dock congestion. The receiving dock had 14 doors, and during the morning peak (roughly 5am to 9am) trucks were queuing in the yard for an average of 47 minutes before getting a door, which was burning detention charges and pushing fresh product later into the day than it should have been. I was asked to figure out why and propose something cheap, because there was no budget for new dock doors.

What I actually did: I pulled six weeks of gate-in and door-assignment timestamps from the yard management system, joined them against the appointment schedule, and discovered that about 60 percent of trucks were arriving outside their booked appointment window, and that the appointment windows themselves were clustered — three big grocery-DC suppliers had all been given 6am slots historically and nobody had revisited it. The congestion was not a capacity problem; it was a scheduling-and-compliance problem. I built a simple discrete-event simulation in Python (using SimPy) to test re-spreading the appointment windows across the 5am-to-9am band and adding a 30-minute grace tolerance before a truck lost its slot. I also drafted a one-page carrier scorecard so the supervisor could show repeat late carriers their own on-time numbers. We piloted the re-spread schedule with the three largest suppliers for three weeks. Average yard dwell dropped from 47 minutes to 19 minutes, detention spend in the pilot window fell by about 38 percent, and — the part I cared about more — the last fresh putaway of the morning moved about 40 minutes earlier, which the QA team liked because product spent less time on the dock. I documented the simulation and handed it off; the supervisor told me they kept using the re-spread schedule after I left.

Warehouse Analytics Intern — Third-Party Logistics (3PL) Provider (prior winter break, 6 weeks)
A shorter winter internship, more analytical and less floor time. I supported the inventory accuracy team across two fulfillment warehouses. The headline problem was that cycle-count accuracy was sitting around 94 percent against a 98 percent target, and mis-picks were generating customer credits. I helped reframe the cycle-count program from a flat random sample to an ABC-weighted plan, so the fast-moving A items (about 15 percent of SKUs but most of the pick volume) got counted weekly instead of quarterly. I wrote the SQL that classified SKUs by pick velocity and pulled the historical adjustment data to show where shrink was actually concentrated. I did not own the rollout — a full-time analyst did — but I built the classification logic and the before/after dashboard. Over the eight weeks after rollout, location-level accuracy on A items moved from roughly 94 percent to 97.5 percent. I was careful in my final readout to flag that two of those eight weeks overlapped a slow post-holiday period, so the improvement might be slightly flattered by lower volume; I did not want to overstate it.

CAPSTONE PROJECT
Semester-long senior capstone (team of four) with the same regional grocery distributor as my summer host. The team built a slotting optimization model for the dry-goods pick module to reduce picker travel distance. My specific contribution was the demand-correlation analysis: I clustered SKUs that were frequently ordered together so that affinity pairs could be slotted near each other, and I validated the clusters against three months of order-line data. The modeled travel-distance reduction was about 12 percent; it has not been physically implemented yet because re-slotting a live module is disruptive, and I am careful to call this a modeled result, not a realized one.

SKILLS
Python (pandas, SimPy for discrete-event simulation), SQL (joins, window functions, enough to be dangerous), Excel modeling, basic Tableau. Methods: discrete-event simulation, ABC inventory classification, cycle counting, SPC control charts, basic linear programming formulation. Comfortable on a warehouse floor and comfortable saying 'I don't know yet, let me pull the data.'

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
An operations rotation where I am close enough to execution to see whether my analysis is right. I would rather own one real, scoped problem end-to-end than touch five things superficially. I am honest about the boundary between what I personally built and what the team or a full-time owner drove, because in my experience that distinction is exactly what separates a useful intern from a resume.

REFERENCES available on request, including my summer inbound operations supervisor.
jd: We are hiring a Supply Chain Operations Intern for a summer rotation embedded with our regional distribution center operations team, reporting to an operations manager. You will own one scoped improvement project (recent intern projects have covered dock scheduling, slotting, and cycle-count accuracy), supported by our planning and analytics teams. Day to day you will spend time on the warehouse floor in the mornings and in data the afternoons. We expect you to pull your own data (SQL or a clean export), frame a hypothesis, and quantify both the problem and your proposed fix. We care a great deal about candidates who can clearly separate what they personally did from what the broader team did, who attach real numbers and timeframes to their claims, and who are honest about the limits of their results. Requirements: pursuing a degree in industrial engineering, supply chain, operations, or a related field; comfort with spreadsheets and basic data tooling; willingness to be on a distribution floor early in the morning. Prior internship or co-op experience in logistics, warehousing, or manufacturing is a plus.

history.q: Walk me through your summer at the grocery distributor — what was the problem you were handed and what was your specific role?
You said about 60 percent of trucks arrived outside their booked window. How did you actually arrive at that 60 percent figure?
Tell me about the simulation. What did you model, and how did you decide the re-spread schedule was worth piloting?
What was the measured result of the pilot, and over what timeframe?
On the 3PL cycle-count project, be precise: what did you personally build versus what the full-time analyst owned?

Here's a scenario for this internship: imagine you're two weeks in, you pull the data on a problem we hand you, and the numbers flatly contradict what the operations manager believes is happening on the floor. What do you do?

candidate_last_answer: Before I answer, can I ask you one clarifying thing — when you say the numbers contradict what the manager believes, do you mean the manager has a strong prior they've stated out loud, or just an informal hunch? Because my move is genuinely different depending on that. Okay, let me give you the version where it's a stated, confident belief, since that's harder. Situation: in my summer internship I hit almost exactly this. The inbound supervisor was convinced the dock congestion was a capacity problem — 'we just don't have enough doors' — and that belief had been driving requests for capital. Task: I'd pulled six weeks of gate-in versus appointment timestamps and the data said capacity wasn't the binding constraint at all; it was off-window arrivals and clustered 6am appointments. So my task was to surface a finding that contradicted my own supervisor without it landing as 'the intern thinks you're wrong.' Action: I did not lead with the conclusion. I first asked him to walk me through how he saw the morning, so I understood his mental model and could check whether I'd missed something operational the data couldn't see — and twice I had missed things, like a recurring equipment outage on door 9. Then I showed him the arrival-versus-appointment distribution as a picture, not a verdict, and asked 'does this match what you see?' Once he was looking at the same chart, the re-spread idea came out of the conversation rather than from me. Result: he backed a three-week pilot, yard dwell went from 47 to 19 minutes, and detention spend in that window dropped about 38 percent — and critically he stopped pushing the capital request for new doors, which he later told me saved him an awkward budget conversation. So to actually answer your question: I'd trust the data enough to act on it, but I'd treat the manager's floor knowledge as a real input that might explain the gap, and I'd never wield the number as a gotcha.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'd never wield the number as a gotcha

expected-yield: pushes past the polished, well-handled summer example to the harder case the candidate dodged — what they would do if, after walking the manager through the chart, the manager still rejected the data and insisted on the capacity story; tests whether they can name the escalation or disagree-and-commit boundary rather than only the version where persuasion succeeds
